item.title: Voters weigh in on Platner sexting scandal in Maine Senate race
item.description: Graham Platner, the presumptive Democratic nominee for Senate in Maine, is pushing forward with his campaign despite a sexting scandal. Caitlin Huey-Burns reports on how it could impact the race and what voters are saying.

item.title: DOJ says it will stop work on Trump's "anti-weaponization" fund
item.description: The Justice Department on Monday said it will comply with a court order temporarily blocking the Trump administration's nearly $1.8 billion "anti-weaponization" fund. Meanwhile, President Trump remains locked in a stalemate with Iran amid peace talks. Weijia Jiang has the latest.

item.title: As Californians head to polls, these are the close races to watch
item.description: In California, voters will head to the polls on Tuesday with tight contests playing out in the gubernatorial and L.A. mayoral races. Matt Gutman reports.

item.title: Senior Iranian officer says return to war with U.S. seems "inevitable"
item.description: Prospects for a U.S.-Iran deal seem to dim as the war between Israel and Hezbollah grinds on despite Trump saying they agreed to stop fighting.

item.title: Eye Opener: White House pauses plans for $1.8 billion "anti-weaponization" fund
item.description: The Trump administration pauses its plan for a nearly $1.8 billion "anti-weaponization" fund that critics called a slush fund for Trump supporters. Plus, tensions flare over a growing Ebola outbreak. All that and all that matters in today's Eye Opener.

item.title: Eye Opener: White House pauses plans for $1.8 billion "anti-weaponization" fund
item.description: The Trump administration pauses its plan for a nearly $1.8 billion "anti-weaponization" fund that critics called a slush fund for Trump supporters. Plus, tensions flare over a growing Ebola outbreak. All that and all that matters in today's Eye Opener.

item.title: Bear goes on rampage in Japan, injuring 4 people
item.description: A record 13 people were killed by bears in Japan last year, and there has been a jump in sightings as the animals emerge hungry from hibernation.

item.title: Russian missile, drone attack on Ukraine kills at least 13, officials say
item.description: Russian missile, drone strikes kill at least 13 people across Ukraine, authorities say, after President Zelenskyy warned Moscow was planning a "massive new strike."

item.title: Ancient coin, mysterious markings found under Notre Dame cathedral
item.description: A team of archaeologists at the iconic cathedral is digging straight down and back in time, to Roman Paris 2,000 years ago.

item.title: Police in Iowa say they think man shot and killed 6 relatives, then himself
item.description: Authorities in Iowa are investigating the fatal shootings of six people they believe were killed by a relative who took his own life when confronted by police.

item.title: Trump says Iran talks continuing after regime threatens "other fronts" in war
item.description: The Iranians' latest move in the war came in response to what it considers U.S. and Israeli ceasefire violations.

item.title: Late California ballots could reshape primary results in closely watched governor's race
item.description: California's governor's race is tightening as many Democratic voters appear to be waiting until the final hours to cast their ballots.

item.title: Vanilla Ice says he'd perform for anybody, including Putin and Iran
item.description: "It's not anything to do with politics. I don't know why they're turning it into politics," Vanilla Ice said of the Freedom 250 concerts planned in Washington, D.C.

item.title: From the archives: Marilyn Monroe, according to those who knew her best
item.description: Monday marks 100 years since the birth of the iconic Hollywood actress Marilyn Monroe. On Aug. 10, 1962, six days after her death at the age of 36, CBS News aired a retrospective special about the legend's life, featuring interviews with friends, mentors and colleagues.

item.title: Northlake police warn of gas pump screw scam
item.description: The Northlake Police Department issued a warning about a new scam at gas stations that is low tech, but effective.

item.title: South Carolina store owner acquitted of murder in 2023 killing of Black teen
item.description: Chikei Rick Chow, 61, shot Cyrus Carmack-Belton in the back after chasing him from his convenience store in Columbia. He maintained he acted to defend his son.

item.title: 6/1: The Takeout with Major Garrett
item.description: Trump says Iran talks are continuing, Iran says otherwise; Graham Platner's wife addresses sexting scandal.

item.title: Platner's wife told campaign about sexually explicit texts he sent other women
item.description: The wife of Democratic Maine Senate candidate Graham Platner told his campaign in 2025 about sexual messages he had sent to other women.

item.title: Vanilla Ice on why he will perform in Freedom 250 concert series: "I'd go play for anybody"
item.description: A number of artists have recently said they will not perform at the upcoming multi-day Great American State Fair at Washington, D.C.'s National Mall. It's being put on by the group Freedom 250, which the BBC says was launched by the Trump administration, with the president appointing its CEO. Rapper Vanilla Ice joins to discuss why he believes the show must go on.

item.title: Sexting scandal latest controversy for Democratic Senate candidate Graham Platner
item.description: The likely Democratic nominee for Senate in Maine, Graham Platner, is under fire after he sent sexually explicit text messages to at least half a dozen women after he got married in 2023. Caitlin Huey-Burns reports.

item.title: Firefly Aerospace CEO on winning $75 million NASA contract to deliver drones to moon's south pole
item.description: NASA is sending drones to the moon to help prepare for a permanent human presence. Firefly Aerospace CEO Jason Kim, whose company just secured a $75 million contract with NASA to deliver four drones to the moon, joins to discuss.

item.title: New drug nearly doubles survival rates in some pancreatic cancer patients, study says
item.description: New research shows a medication called daraxonrasib is helping people with advanced pancreatic cancer live longer.

item.title: North Carolina officer fired over video of violent arrest turns himself in
item.description: Former Shelby County police officer Karson Hyder has been charged with one count of assault inflicting serious injury after video appeared to show him repeatedly punching a woman.

item.title: TMZ DC managing editors on their approach to Capitol Hill
item.description: One of Hollywood's most powerful and feared tabloids is making waves in Washington, D.C. The managing editors of TMZ DC, Jacob Wasserman and Charlie Cotton, join "The Takeout" to discuss their coverage.

item.title: Descendant of Betsy Ross donates sewing table to her house in Philly
item.description: A descendant of Betsy Ross is donating her sewing table to her house in Philadelphia and taking a historic journey of his own this summer.

item.title: 6/1: CBS Evening News
item.description: DOJ stopping work on President Trump's "anti-weaponization" fund; fired North Carolina cop arrested after video shows him beating woman.

item.title: About 1 in 4 professionals is stuck in a mid-career stall, study finds
item.description: Workers who go at least five years without a promotion or meaningful raise can miss out on thousands of dollars in earnings, researchers found.

item.title: What to know about the major student loan changes coming July 1
item.description: The changes will affect how much students and their parents can borrow, as well as their repayment options.

item.title: U.S. political commentators say U.K. barred them "for criticizing Israel"
item.description: Left-wing streamers Cenk Uygur and Hasan Piker say Britain's government has denied them entry over their criticism of Israel.

item.title: Can hackers pull your fingerprints from photos online? Experts explain.
item.description: Claims have circulated on social media that fingerprints can be pulled from photos featuring peace signs, but experts say the risk to the average person is low.
